preamble: Rhotacism is the process where a consonant (typically a voiced sibilant [z] from [s]) changes into a rhotic sound [r]. This is a classic example of regular sound change in Latin and Germanic historical linguistics.

examples.note: Intervocalic [s] > [z] > [r] in Old Latin (c. 4th century BC).
Remnant of Rhotacism via Verner's Law (s > z > r).

sources: Lass, R. (1997). Historical Linguistics and Language Change.
